facts.fact: Lamport timestamps enable partial ordering of events in distributed systems without synchronized clocks; each process maintains a counter that increments with every local event, and receives the maximum of its counter and any received timestamp
Raft leader election: followers have an election timeout (typically 150-300ms) for receiving heartbeats; if no heartbeat arrives, the follower becomes a candidate and initiates an election
Raft log replication: leader receives AppendEntries RPCs from followers; entry is committed once replicated to majority of servers; followers append entries and acknowledge success
etcd default heartbeat interval is 100ms; default election timeout is 1000ms (10× the heartbeat); missing up to 9 consecutive heartbeats allows leader to maintain role
For etcd across multiple data centers, heartbeat interval should be ~0.5-1.5× round-trip time (RTT); election timeout upper limit is 50s for globally-distributed clusters
Idempotent consumer pattern: consumer records processed message IDs in database; detects duplicates by querying database before processing; works with at-least-once message delivery
Kafka exactly-once semantics requires two guarantees: idempotent production (broker deduplicates via producer ID + sequence number) and transactional processing (atomic produce-consume-process)
Exactly-once semantics incurs significant performance overhead; two-phase commit for transactional messages results in lower throughput and increased latency compared to at-least-once
CockroachDB uses Raft consensus protocol for range replication; each Raft command is persisted to log, replicated to majority via AppendEntries RPC, then committed to storage engine
CockroachDB uses Hybrid Logical Clocks (HLC) to assign transaction timestamps; enables multi-version concurrency control (MVCC) and serializability without synchronized physical clocks
Clock skew on the internet commonly reaches tens of minutes; even in controlled distributed computing environments with NTP, significant clock skew at millisecond resolution is common
facts.note: Practical observation: don't trust clock synchronization

facts.fact: Clock skew of few milliseconds is often acceptable; skew reaching tens of milliseconds visibly distorts distributed trace timelines

Network partitions split a cluster into majority and minority partitions; only majority partition can hold quorum and accept writes; minority partition must stop accepting writes to prevent split-brain
For n-node cluster, quorum size is n/2 + 1; in 5-node cluster partitioned 3-2, only the 3-node partition can achieve quorum

The eight fallacies of distributed computing (Deutsch, 1994; Gosling added 8th in 1997): network reliability, zero latency, infinite bandwidth, security, topology stability, single admin, zero transport cost, homogeneity
Fallacy: 'Bandwidth is infinite' is approximately true for application-layer traffic within a cloud region in 2024, but false for inter-region and internet traffic
Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) has quadratic messaging complexity O(n²), limiting scalability; used in permissioned blockchains (Hyperledger Fabric) but not at internet scale
PBFT requires f < n/3 (fewer than one-third faulty nodes); used in aerospace (Boeing 777/787 flight control) and blockchain consensus (Bitcoin proof-of-work analogue)

Two-phase commit (2PC) protocol: Phase 1 (prepare) — coordinator polls all participants if they can commit; Phase 2 (commit) — if all agree, coordinator broadcasts commit; if any refuse, broadcasts rollback
2PC is synchronous and blocking; if coordinator crashes after Phase 1 but before Phase 2, participants hold locks indefinitely, blocking progress (part of CAP theorem tradeoff)
Vector clocks allow partial causal ordering of events; A→B if and only if VC(A) < VC(B) component-wise; distinguish causality vs concurrency (Lamport timestamps cannot)
Vector clock overhead: each process maintains array of size n (number of processes); grows linearly with cluster size; limited to systems with <100 nodes in practice
Cloudflare June 20, 2024 incident: latent bug in Traffic Manager caused 0.5%-2.1% error rate; triggered exception recovery loop at 18:17 UTC, repeated 18:35-18:57 UTC blocking traffic routing actions
Cloudflare November 2, 2023 control plane outage (3.6 hours): lasted 11/2 11:43 UTC to 11/4 04:25 UTC; failover to European disaster recovery sites recovered service
AWS us-east-1 August 21, 2025 incident: traffic surge caused congestion on Cloudflare-AWS links; high latency, packet loss, failures to origin resolved via close Cloudflare-AWS collaboration
Kubernetes split-brain DNS: on-premises DNS serving stale IPs while cloud DNS has updated service IPs; DNS TTL caching propagates inconsistency for hours despite quick fix

examples.scenario: Boeing 777 aircraft flight control system uses Byzantine fault tolerance to tolerate sensor failures; multiple redundant computers vote on control surface commands to mask single node failures
examples.metric: f < n/3 Byzantine nodes tolerated; critical safety system

examples.scenario: Kafka financial services consumer processes payment messages with idempotent consumer pattern; duplicate message ID detected via database constraint prevents double-charging customer
examples.metric: At-least-once delivery with deduplication achieves exactly-once semantics

examples.scenario: CockroachDB geo-distributed transaction: New York node initiates write, coordinator assigns HLC timestamp, replicates via Raft to Toronto and London replicas; majority commit succeeds, returns to client

Kubernetes etcd cluster experiences network partition: 5 nodes split 3-2; 3-node partition elects new leader via Raft (election timeout 1s, heartbeat 100ms); 2-node partition cannot achieve quorum, blocks writes
examples.metric: Quorum: 3 out of 5 nodes; partition recovery time ~1s per failed heartbeat

examples.scenario: Cloudflare June 2024 outage: Traffic Manager exception triggered recovery loop every 30-40 seconds; cascading failure blocked new traffic routing decisions for 40 minutes until human intervention reset service

Multi-cloud Kubernetes on GCP + on-premises: on-premises DNS authority still serving old IP (10.0.1.50) for service; GCP Cloud DNS serving new IP (35.201.0.15) for same domain; requests randomly routed to both
examples.metric: Split-brain DNS causes ~50% failure rate due to random routing

analogies.analogy: Like a library checkout system where each desk maintains a running count. When a person checks out a book, they write down their desk's current count. If Person A (desk 1, count=5) tells Person B (desk 2, count=3) about a checkout, desk 2 updates to at least count=6. Later checkouts have higher counts, proving ordering.
analogies.why_it_works: No synchronized clocks needed; relative ordering emerges from increments and message passing

analogies.analogy: A classroom where the teacher (leader) sends periodic attendance checks (heartbeats). If students don't hear from the teacher for 1 second (election timeout), they assume the teacher left, hold an election, and the student with the most votes becomes the new teacher. The new teacher starts sending attendance checks.
analogies.why_it_works: Timeout-based detection is simple; majority voting prevents fractured leadership

analogies.analogy: A 5-person board split by communication failure into 3 people and 2 people. Only the group of 3 can make binding decisions (majority). The group of 2 sits idle until reconnection because they know they're outnumbered and any decision they make would be wrong.
analogies.why_it_works: Guarantees at most one partition can have quorum; prevents split-brain decisions

analogies.analogy: A wedding ceremony with a coordinator (judge). Phase 1: Judge asks all parties 'Do you take this person?' Each person mentally prepares their answer and signals readiness. Phase 2: Judge finalizes — everyone says 'I do' simultaneously, or the judge cancels the ceremony if anyone says no. No halfway marriages.
analogies.why_it_works: Forces all participants to agree before any commits; prevents inconsistent state

analogies.analogy: A restaurant kitchen that writes every completed order to a 'finished orders' board before sending it to the customer. If the waiter crashes carrying the plate, the kitchen checks the board, sees the order already finished, and doesn't remake it.
analogies.why_it_works: Duplicate detection via external state prevents re-processing; safe with at-least-once delivery

analogies.analogy: Lamport = a single global sequence number (A sent message 5, B received it and bumped to 6). Vector = each person has a memory of all their conversations (A=[5,0,0] bumps to [6,0,0] when sending to B; B=[0,3,0] receives and becomes [6,4,0]). Vector lets you detect causality; Lamport only orders everything globally.
analogies.why_it_works: Vector captures per-process knowledge; Lamport throws away causality info by collapsing to a single integer

analogies.analogy: Three friends trying to meet at a cafe: Alice's watch is 2 minutes fast, Bob's is 3 minutes slow, Charlie's is correct. They text each other 'See you at 3:00 PM.' Alice arrives at 2:58, Bob at 3:03, Charlie at 3:00. They miss each other. Add NTP (trusted clock server in the building), and only 10ms drift remains—everyone arrives within 1 minute.
analogies.why_it_works: Illustrates drift and why NTP helps but doesn't guarantee zero skew; logical clocks avoid the problem entirely

key_numbers.context: Timeout for followers to detect leader failure and start election
Interval at which leader broadcasts keepalive to followers

key_numbers.metric: etcd leader election timeout to heartbeat ratio
key_numbers.context: Tolerance: can lose up to 9 heartbeats before election triggers

key_numbers.metric: etcd election timeout upper limit for geo-distributed clusters
key_numbers.context: For globally-distributed deployments with high latency

key_numbers.metric: etcd recommended heartbeat interval for high-latency networks
key_numbers.context: Should track network latency to avoid unnecessary elections

key_numbers.metric: Clock skew visibility threshold in distributed traces
key_numbers.context: Above this, clock skew noticeably distorts trace timelines
Without careful synchronization, significant drift is common
Minimum nodes needed for consensus; prevents split-brain in any partition
PBFT can tolerate arbitrary failures in fewer than 1/3 of nodes
Limits PBFT to small clusters; scales poorly

key_numbers.metric: Cloudflare June 2024 error rate during partition
key_numbers.context: Cascading exception loop causing traffic manager failures

key_numbers.metric: Cloudflare November 2023 control plane outage duration
key_numbers.value: 2 hours 42 minutes (11:43 UTC to 14:25 UTC on day 1; lasted into day 3)
key_numbers.context: Multi-day partition requiring failover to DR sites

key_numbers.metric: Kafka exactly-once vs at-least-once performance cost
key_numbers.value: Lower throughput and increased latency (exact % varies by workload)
key_numbers.context: 2PC overhead; many systems trade off exactly-once for performance

common_misconceptions.misconception: Synchronized clocks solve distributed systems problems
common_misconceptions.reality: Perfect clock synchronization is impossible (Deutsch fallacy: 'latency is zero'). Even NTP-synchronized clocks drift tens of milliseconds. Use logical clocks (Lamport, vector) instead.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Relying on physical clocks for ordering causes subtle causality bugs; logical timestamps are provably correct

common_misconceptions.misconception: At-least-once message delivery is good enough — idempotent consumers don't matter
common_misconceptions.reality: At-least-once guarantees duplicates will occur (retries, crashes, rebalancing). Consumer must be idempotent or financial/data loss will happen. Don't skip deduplication.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Duplicate payments, inventory corruption, or data inconsistency

common_misconceptions.reality: Exactly-once (via 2PC or transactional messages) incurs significant overhead: lower throughput, higher latency. Usually only worth it for critical operations (payments, unique ID generation).
common_misconceptions.consequence: Unnecessarily slow systems; most real systems use at-least-once + idempotency for speed

common_misconceptions.misconception: Network partitions are rare — assume the network is reliable
common_misconceptions.reality: Network partitions happen regularly in production (ISP failures, misconfigured firewalls, DNS glitches). Fallacy #1 of distributed computing. Plan for partition recovery.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Unplanned downtime, data inconsistency, split-brain scenarios

common_misconceptions.misconception: Two-phase commit guarantees atomicity across all systems
common_misconceptions.reality: 2PC guarantees atomic commit of the transaction, but not atomicity of side effects. If a participant crashes after Phase 1 but before Phase 2, locks are held indefinitely (blocking problem).
common_misconceptions.consequence: Cascading failures, system hangs, resource exhaustion

common_misconceptions.reality: Quorum consensus prevents one partition from accepting writes; it sacrifices availability (minority partition goes offline) to preserve consistency. Doesn't make the network reliable.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Minority partition becomes unavailable; applications must handle graceful degradation

common_misconceptions.reality: Leader election takes time: followers must timeout (150-300ms default in Raft), become candidates, collect votes, etc. During election, no leader exists; system is unavailable.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Write latency spikes or failures during leader failure recovery

common_misconceptions.misconception: Clock skew doesn't matter for application-level logic
common_misconceptions.reality: Clock skew corrupts: transaction timestamps, event ordering, timeout calculations, lease expiration (nodes think their lease expired when it hasn't). Logical clocks fix this.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Subtle bugs where causality is inverted; hard to debug in production

common_misconceptions.reality: Split-brain DNS happens: on-premises DNS and cloud DNS both claim authority; caching propagates inconsistency for hours. Service discovery can route to stale IPs.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Requests routed to wrong service instance, mysterious timeouts, multi-cloud failures

common_misconceptions.misconception: Lamport timestamps uniquely order all events
common_misconceptions.reality: Lamport timestamps create total ordering by adding process ID as tiebreaker, but lose causality information. Vector clocks preserve causality; concurrent events have incomparable timestamps.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Application assumes false ordering; concurrent writes appear ordered when they're actually independent
